title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Superintendent Spence discuss the murder of a thirteen-year-old girl. Poirot describes a cruel method of killing involving a trick to bite an apple, which suggests a perpetrator who might have a history of child abuse. Spence notes that the local community is unstable with high population turnover, though some residents like Miss Emery and Dr. Ferguson are long-term inhabitants. Spence identifies two potential suspects from the party age group: Nicholas Lanchester, a seventeen or eighteen-year-old man, and a person named Desmond, who was previously held in custody due to a psychological report. Spence also considers the possibility that an outsider entered through an open door or window.

The investigation is driven by a claim made by a girl named Joyce. Poirot recounts to Spence that Joyce, during a conversation about Mrs. Oliver’s books, mentioned having seen a murder once. While Mrs. Oliver and Spence initially view this as a tall tale or exaggeration, Poirot argues that the girl's death shortly after making the claim suggests a causal link. If Joyce had witnessed a crime years prior—perhaps an accident she did not realize was intentional—the discussion of murder may have triggered her memory, prompting the killer to act. Spence notes that the party included various adults, such as Dr. Ferguson and the parish priest, as well as approximately fourteen children. Poirot suggests the murderer may be someone who appears unremarkable and is now acting out of shock because they have been discovered.
